name: Jenny's Factors of Soil Formation Oceanography Theory
mechanism: Jenny's Factors of Soil Formation in pedology operates through the interaction of soil profile as the fundamental unit of analysis. Variation arises through climate, organisms, relief, parent material, time (CLORPT), creating a diverse population of candidates that face differential pressures. The selection mechanism—weathering and biological processes differentiate horizons—determines which variants persist and propagate, while fitness is measured by soil maturity (horizon development). This process is bounded by landscape position, geological time scale, establishing the conditions under which the mechanism produces reliable outcomes. The structural insight is that any domain exhibiting analogous unit-variation-selection dynamics should show comparable emergent patterns regardless of substrate.
application: When transplanted to oceanography, the mechanism from pedology suggests that the core units of oceanography undergo an analogous evolutionary process. The variation mechanism (climate, organisms, relief, parent material, time (CLORPT)) maps onto the generative processes in oceanography, while the selection pressure (weathering and biological processes differentiate horizons) corresponds to the evaluative forces that determine which innovations, strategies, or structures persist in the target domain. This cross-domain mapping predicts that oceanography systems will exhibit phase transitions, path dependencies, and fitness landscape topologies that mirror those observed in pedology, but expressed through domain-specific substrates and timescales. The boundary conditions (landscape position, geological time scale) constrain where the analogy holds and where it breaks down.
prediction: Systems in oceanography designed using pedology structural principles will show 28% increase in output quality compared to conventional approaches, measurable across a sample of n=100 cases over a 18-month observation period using domain-standard assessment instruments.

name: Quadratic Reciprocity Paleontology Protocol
mechanism: Quadratic Reciprocity in number theory operates through the interaction of pair of odd primes as the fundamental unit of analysis. Variation arises through Legendre symbol (p|q) and (q|p), creating a diverse population of candidates that face differential pressures. The selection mechanism—reciprocity law constrains mutual quadratic residuosity—determines which variants persist and propagate, while fitness is measured by predictive symmetry between primes. This process is bounded by odd primes, modular arithmetic, establishing the conditions under which the mechanism produces reliable outcomes. This cross-domain mapping predicts that paleontology systems will exhibit phase transitions, path dependencies, and fitness landscape topologies that mirror those observed in number theory, but expressed through domain-specific substrates and timescales. The boundary conditions (odd primes, modular arithmetic) constrain where the analogy holds and where it breaks down.
prediction: Systems in paleontology designed using number theory structural principles will show 28% increase in output quality compared to conventional approaches, measurable across a sample of n=60 cases over a 12-month observation period using domain-standard assessment instruments.

name: Source-Sink Carbon Allocation Geomorphology Calculus
mechanism: Source-Sink Carbon Allocation in mycorrhizal ecology operates through the interaction of photosynthate carbon packet allocated belowground as the fundamental unit of analysis. Variation arises through host plants vary carbon allocation to fungal partners based on nutrient return and light availability, creating a diverse population of candidates that face differential pressures. The selection mechanism—fungi delivering more phosphorus and nitrogen receive preferential carbon; low-return partners are starved—determines which variants persist and propagate, while fitness is measured by proportional carbon share captured by a fungal partner. This process is bounded by host photosynthetic capacity, number of competing fungal partners, soil nutrient status, establishing the conditions under which the mechanism produces reliable outcomes. The structural insight is that any domain exhibiting analogous unit-variation-selection dynamics should show comparable emergent patterns regardless of substrate.
application: When transplanted to geomorphology, the mechanism from mycorrhizal ecology suggests that the core units of geomorphology undergo an analogous evolutionary process. The variation mechanism (host plants vary carbon allocation to fungal partners based on nutrient return and light availability) maps onto the generative processes in geomorphology, while the selection pressure (fungi delivering more phosphorus and nitrogen receive preferential carbon; low-return partners are starved) corresponds to the evaluative forces that determine which innovations, strategies, or structures persist in the target domain. This cross-domain mapping predicts that geomorphology systems will exhibit phase transitions, path dependencies, and fitness landscape topologies that mirror those observed in mycorrhizal ecology, but expressed through domain-specific substrates and timescales. The boundary conditions (host photosynthetic capacity, number of competing fungal partners, soil nutrient status) constrain where the analogy holds and where it breaks down.
prediction: Systems in geomorphology designed using mycorrhizal ecology structural principles will show 35% improvement in resilience measures compared to conventional approaches, measurable across a sample of n=150 cases over a 8-month observation period using domain-standard assessment instruments.
